speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape five, the interview with Nathan Krieger. The date is December 4, 1995. Before we continue on your journey back to Bochnia and to Tonia, tell me a little bit more. I'm curious, while you were in Auschwitz, were there are occasions when people were able to get together to talk? You had been religious before the war. Did you have occasion to have any kind of religious experiences? Did they celebrate any holidays in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. You were always a group together, which we had guys, which they remember exactly the Jewish calendar and the Jewish date. And we always tried. I remember going to work and coming back. We used to stay in line and give back-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --shovels and the other tools, what you worked the whole day. There was always a guy who would say, mincha time. And if they would catch you praying, they would kill you. But we would stay in line, and we had to pray not to move your lips, or whatever. And then he always picked a time when you had to say kedusha, that the capo shouldn't watch us and the SS should be in front. So you used to say a little bit louder, because everybody had to repeat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were always somebody to go ahead, and they pushed the next guy, kedusha, kedusha, you know? And that was-- that was such a good feeling that you didn't lose your spirit, you know, with all the problems what we had.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would say again, me especially, I never lost hope in God. And that helped me a lot. And we knew knew exactly when was Friday, when was Shabbos, when was Rosh Chodesh, when was a holiday. You know, I remember one Pesach, which-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was in [NON-ENGLISH] already. So I made up my mind, I have plenty potatoes to eat. I didn't have to eat bread. I'll survive. So I said, if God helped me to come in here, I lived on coffee and potatoes through the whole Pesach. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to talk about it. I helped people outside to bring in food, and this and that. I said, today Pesach. No bread-- potatoes. Pesach. You know-- how do you know it's Pesach? We had a way, you know. And that was really a spiritual uplift for you-- helped you physically to survive. Who were some of these people who were involved in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there are two brothers-- Katz, Moishe Katz. And then one is a [NON-ENGLISH]. [INAUDIBLE] I met them at a bar mitzvah in Woodmere here. And we were talking-- remember, we were standing in line. He was much younger. He was a young kid at that time. It was such a good feeling. We met at a bar mitzvah, and we were talking, you know, about those days. And we tried as much as we could. Unfortunately, the older one, the very frum, didn't survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There's a lot of questions, but we're not allowed to ask those questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any religious personalities in camp? Any rebbes in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no rebbes, because the rebbes were older people, you know, and whoever came with a beard to Auschwitz, they didn't select, you know. When he came to the selection-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --as far as I know, they were always--

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to the Belzer rebbe, the one who gave you the blessing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Belzer rebbe, I remember being in the Bochnia ghetto. And then I was told when I was there that somebody's going to come and take him out. They shaved his beard. And they put him on a Hungarian uniform. And I think Hungarian or German officers came with a truck, and they took him from Bochnia through the Polish-Hungarian border. And they smuggled him out from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was able to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He survived. And I think from there he went to Israel. But I'm here today because of the Belzer rebbe, because whatever happened to me, he said, it can be, because he was a tzadik, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I said, the Belzer rebbe gave me a blessing that I'm going to live through the war. I said, nothing is going to happen to me. Never lost hope. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think that's the reason I'm here to tell the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back now to after you were on your way. How long did it take you to get to Bochnia from Czechoslovakia, from Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think around three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you got to Bochnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a guy there, which he gave up his house. His name was [INAUDIBLE]. And all the people what came, no matter how many people came, they had room. It was like a Jewish committee. We were there. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --one morning I was waking up, and they told me that Tonia's here, because she was liberated in Germany. from [NON-ENGLISH] which she was, was Ravensbruck. And she came. That's how we met. We met in the Bochnia ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miracle. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We started up a new life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was b'shert. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And-- --we had a friend, which lives in Milwaukee now. He found out. He was in Russia during the war. And he finds a good friend of mine. We belonged to the Zionist organization together. And he find out that I am alive, so he came to Bochnia. And they lived in Lodz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came here and he says, you're not going to stay here. Come. You're going to go with me. Taking you in for a partner. Says, you are a tailor, and I'm buying suits and selling suits and everything. So he took me in for a partner. He rented an apartment for me in Lodz. And we started out to make a new life.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until I was almost killed in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Going from Krakow to Lodz, you had to make a living, so either you bought cigarettes in Krakow and you brought them into Lodz or to Warsaw, or you bought yeast, which is called, in Jewish, heyvn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, I remember, I had two suitcases with heyvn. I see, and I'm walking, and I see a guy with fruit. And I said to him in Polish, [POLISH]. So he answered me, [POLISH]. And I said to him, where you're going? He said, to Lodz. And I said, you going to take me? Says, I'll take you, but I have no money for gas.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he takes out papers and showed me, he's the [NON-ENGLISH], the head of the company. And he promised me, soon I'm going to come there. He's going to give back-- he's going to give me back the money. And it was very dangerous to go by train, because they could catch you, confiscate you, put you in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of actions against people during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was black market, which, you know, they were stealing, you know, whatever. It wasn't-- it wasn't legal. So I took a chance. I had money with me, and I lent it to him. And when we came to Lodz, he called up, and they came over with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --and they took us right away, and he gave me back the money, and he gave me a letter. Whenever his company-- whenever I need something from his company, they should take me. So I had a free ride. I used to go. Didn't worry. I bought, came back, sold it. Soon I came there. There was no-- because here they used to bring from Hungary to Krakow, was not too far. But to Lodz and Warsaw, they couldn't get that stuff, because the Polish people used to make whiskey out that yeast, and they need it for baking purposes and everything. It was a good thing. And once we're driving, he said to put in four ton fruit. So he put in five or six. So every few miles we had a flat. And he said to take us six hours. It took us, God knows, 18 hours or something. And it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --in Polish they call it [POLISH] That was Armia Krajowa, a bunch of anti-Semites. It was terrible. After the war, they killed so many Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were sitting with the truck, and fixing the tires and everything. So they're coming out with the machine guns and everything, and they say in Polish, any Jews within you? So he said no. So he said, show me the papers. So everybody takes out papers, and I had the paper as a survivor. So I put it underneath the blanket.

speaker: interviewee
text: And right away, it was hot. It was summertime, so I went ahead and I put something on top. They shouldn't see my number.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, where are your papers? I said, I didn't have them. I left them in  the house. So they say, you must be a Jew. So he goes ahead, and he says, do you think that I'm going to hire Jews to work? I don't have enough Polish people to hire? I'm going to hire him to work? So they started up with him. So he goes ahead, and he takes out a paper from his pocket, and he shows it to him. So everybody stood up and they saluted to him. He was the head of those anti-Semites. So he goes to me like this-- you remember you saved my life? I had five trucks. If you wouldn't lend me the money to put in-- they were sitting and drinking. So they-- they drank all the money. He said, I would be fired. Probably I would be shot, or whatever. And he saved my life, because if he wouldn't do that, they would kill me on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Driving on the road there, I never want to see a scene like this, how many people were dead on the road. They stop the cars and the trucks, and whoever was--

speaker: interviewer
text: On this journey that you're describing, there were dead people on the road?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Whoever was Jewish, they took them out and killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know that these Armia Krajowa--

speaker: interviewee
text: Armia Krajowa, that was in Polish, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --was killing people along the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's after the war. I came back home, and my wife says, what happened to you? I'm so scared. So I tell her the journey. And she says, never mind. I had all blisters, because over there, to fix a tire, it's not [INAUDIBLE]. You had to pump.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was working like dogs. And I said to my wife, you know what? That's it. I had enough. We have to leave. So then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a bunch of guys, which that one friend would help me, and his friends. And they-- they all spoke Russian. And they had got acquainted with the Russian general. And he took it out from Poland as prisoners of war to Germany, to East Germany. From East Germany, we had to smuggle to West Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I was in DP camp for four years in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Landsberg [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone come with you besides Tonia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where, here?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, to Landsberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: To Landsberg? Yeah, my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many friends were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were two guys. One lives in Milwaukee. His brother passed away, lived in Australia. And then I have a very good friend in San Diego, which I go and visit him. [INAUDIBLE]. You saw the pictures there with the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were together in Landsberg with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were together. A few went to Landsberg, and a few went to Freimann, which was 30, 40 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work while you were in Landsberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Black market. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was lucky. They arrested me once with cigarettes-- a few guys, not only me. And there was the American governor. He was in charge, you know. And by that time, I'm talking about 1945, there was no Polish supervision. Everything was American.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to my friend, I said to him, do me a favor. Go and tell my wife that I am arrested, I don't know when I'm going to come home, in Polish. So this governor let everybody out. And I was on the end. And at the end, he starts to talk to me in Polish. He starts to talk to me in Polish. He said don't worry about it. I'll give you back everything what I confiscated. My father comes from Poland. So I had a free ride with him. You want to-- Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, you know, we became very good friends because of my Polish language. So I had a free hand a little bit, so I-- my brother [INAUDIBLE] came here to America. So he says to me, enjoy yourself, because if you come here, don't worry about it. They're looking for tailors. You'll do OK. Just live and enjoy yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I bought a car. I bought a motorcycle.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a good time, waiting to get papers to come to America. And God bless America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: March the 3, 1949. And I just-- I looked for old pictures, and I found the list. So I have one Schindler's list, and I have a list with all the people, what they came on the same boat. And my name and my wife's name is there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I wish she should be here to tell her story. We have wonderful children, wonderful grandchildren. She left me a nice family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: She got sick in 1980. She had a few good years, and then she was sick for five years. And she passed away in 1989. [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] everything to live for. [CRYING] Nice home. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children live nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: They both live in [? Great Neck. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they visit often? Do they visit often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go there for every Shabbos. They are busy. The kids are in school. Thank God, all the grandchildren are there, [INAUDIBLE]. Jewish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your youngest is named for--

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave them a good upbringing. I was busy making a living, and she raised the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son had a child after she died, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son had a little girl after your wife died. They named her Tonia, I remember. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there anything-- Why couldn't she be here to enjoy them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you wanted to say to your children, now that it's so many years after the war, so that they should [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: What shall I say? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They should never leave hope. My children are giving in them a good upbringing, Jewish upbringing. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] If God helped their zayde to come out from that hell, I have one wish to them-- never leave hope, and God will help  you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Krieger, for sharing your story. Who do we see in this picture, Mr. Krieger?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father, when when came here to the United States in 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here here after the war. My relatives were here. Found his brother, my uncle and cousins, and they gave me that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture, Mr. Krieger?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother in the middle, her name Malka Krieger, her maiden name Beldengreen. Below is my brother, Moshe. And top to your left is my sister Mecha.

speaker: interviewer
text: The youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she's the oldest, on top, I said, on the left. And then it's me. Then there's my sister Sarah. And then there's my sister. Below Sarah is Mindel. And then to your left, on the bottom, is my younger sister, Feigel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must be in the middle of the '30s-- in the '30s, '33, '34, '35. I don't know exactly. There's no date on the other side of the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right is my oldest sister, Chana. And to the left is the younger sister, Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to have this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think from relatives in New York. They gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my oldest brother, Joseph. My best friend, to the left, is Honik Swasenstiel. And that's me to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell their name, also, Mr.-- and their first name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Tonia's parents, Abraham and Malka Schon.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be in 1937, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's the dog?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's their dog. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They always had a dog in their house for protection.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Tonia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1936 or 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to have this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her sister was in hiding by gentile people, and she probably had that picture, and she gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture? What is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a picture taken in the Zionist organization, [NON-ENGLISH]. In the middle is the leader from the organization. His name is-- was Yechiel Symchowicz. To the left, it's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're wearing a uniform, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was the assistant. And there was my best friend, Honik Swasenstiel. And that was our friend, [INAUDIBLE]. And to the right is Kuchler. And to the extreme right is one I don't remember the name. To the extreme right is his brother, Honik Swasenstiel. On top is John Farver. He lives in Milwaukee. In the middle is [INAUDIBLE]. And then it's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who survived the war from this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two people.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guy what lives in Milwaukee. His name is John Farver. On the right, the extreme right on the top. It's only two people alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the leader from the Zionist organization, Yechiel Symchowicz. And he was the leader from the underground. He got killed by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the left is me. He got killed in the end of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wisnicz Nowy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's going on in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in the [NON-ENGLISH] organizations. To the right and the bottom is me. To  the left, hanging down, is my friend--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you all in a tree?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. We were young.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Way before the war. That must have been [INAUDIBLE] 1934, 1935. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The one to the left who's hanging down is my friend Honik Swasenstiel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is Nathan [INAUDIBLE], Regina Miller, Libec Beldengreen and his friend. Then is Bernard Krieger and Gusta-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --Kaufas. Then it's Tonia and me. The only people left after the war was Tonia and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture on the top is Halle Weiner, my wife's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Wisnicz Nowy in 1942, when we had to wear the armbands to know that we are Jewish, with the Jewish star. On the bottom right-- on the bottom to the right is Tonia's brother, Mayer. Then it's my brother-in-law's brother, Andrew Weiner's brother. And then on top left is their cousin, [INAUDIBLE] Weiner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Tonia's brother, Mayer Schon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this is taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Wisnicz, before they [INAUDIBLE]. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to Mayer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayer came to Auschwitz with us, and then he was sent to a different camp. And then I was told from my friend that he was shot, April 24, 1945, a day before the Americans came in to liberate the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a friend told us approximately where he was shot--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mayer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayer, so right after the war. So we went there, and we put on a matzeiva, a monument. This is Mayer Schon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's standing next to the monument?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Tonia, my wife and his sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Tonia, myself, taken in 1974, in Marilyn's wedding. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me a thing when to-- Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the whole family, [NON-ENGLISH]. That was taken on Jonathan's bar mitzvah in 1993. To my left is my son, my daughter-in-law, Tonia, Jonathan, Anna, and Daniella. To my right is David and Marilyn, Michael and Dora, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Tonia and myself, taken in 1946 in Landsberg [INAUDIBLE] at the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Krieger.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I thank you for letting telling me the story.
